title: Evolution and epidemiology of pks<sup>+</sup>Klebsiella pneumoniae: Global and local insights.
author_string: Zhao, Yunan, Zhao, Yizheng, Shen, Qi, Hu, Xiaolei, Zhou, Simeng et al.
abstract: This study aimed to investigate the epidemiological characteristics and genomic evolutionary mechanisms of pks-positive Klebsiella pneumoniae (pks <sup>+</sup> KPN), providing theoretical insights for infection prevention and control strategies. A total of 873 non-duplicate K. pneumoniae isolates collected between 2016 and 2022 at the Fifth Affiliated Hospital of Wenzhou Medical University were screened for pks <sup>+</sup> strains via PCR targeting clbA, clbB, clbN, and clbQ. Clonal structures of pks<sup>+</sup> and pks<sup>-</sup> strains were determined by MLST and KL typing, and virulence gene profiles (peg344, iucA, rmpA, rmpA2, iroB) were analyzed to compare the two populations. Global distribution patterns of pks <sup>+</sup> KPN were analyzed using data from the Bacterial and Viral Bioinformatics Resource Center (BV-BRC). Pan-genomic analysis via the IPGA platform and a phylogenetic tree was constructed. Core-genome comparative analysis identified lineage-specific genes in dominant pks<sup>+</sup> strains, and KEGG enrichment revealed their putative biological functions. Statistical analyses were performed using SPSS 26.0, with p < 0.05 considered statistically significant. Among 873 clinical isolates, 105 (12.03 %) were pks <sup>+</sup> KPN, predominantly isolated from infectious disease and surgical departments. The pks island prevalence was significantly higher in non-carbapenem-resistant strains (25.45 %) than in carbapenem-resistant strains (1.04 %) (χ<sup>2</sup> = 125.57, p < 0.001). pks <sup>+</sup> strains exhibited higher virulence gene carriage rates (p < 0.05) and infected younger patients (57.97 ± 14.90 vs. 64.18 ± 17.29 years, t = 3.46, p = 0.001). pks <sup>+</sup> isolates showed a conserved clonal structure dominated by ST23-KL1 (66.7 %), while pks<sup>-</sup> strains displayed greater heterogeneity with 37 distinct ST-KL types. Analysis of 706 global pks <sup>+</sup> KPN revealed that ST23 (45.18 %), ST11 (15.72 %), and ST258 (15.16 %) are the predominant clonal lineages. Phylogenetic construction delineated five evolutionarily distinct clades among pks<sup>+</sup> strains. Comparative core genome analysis identified 245 lineage-associated genes, of which 96 were shared among dominant strains. Functional enrichment (KEGG) demonstrated that these conserved genes are significantly enriched in the galactose metabolism. This study systematically elucidates the clinical epidemiology, clonal dissemination patterns, and genomic evolution of pks <sup>+</sup> KPN, identifying ST23-KL1 as the dominant clone. The findings reveal that galactose metabolism may enhance the adaptability of dominant strains, thereby driving their global spread.
